perturbation.original_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be determined in accordance with the laws of the State of Michigan.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be determined at the sole discretion of the Transporter.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to Michigan law and granting the Transporter sole discretion introduces a legal risk by potentially violating state laws regarding fair dispute resolution and contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL 445.903(1)(t) prohibits unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in trade or commerce, including inserting terms in a contract that grant one party sole discretion to resolve disputes, potentially overriding consumer rights and legal protections afforded by Michigan law. The modified text directly contradicts this by removing the application of Michigan law and granting sole discretion to the Transporter, which could be deemed an unfair or unconscionable practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 331 of 1976 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT (EXCERPT) Act 331 of 1976 445.903 Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in conduct of trade or commerce; rules; applicability of subsection (1)(hh). Sec. 3. (1) Unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce are unlawful and are defined as follows: (a) Causing a probability of confusion or misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (b) Using deceptive representations or deceptive designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (c) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have. (d) Representing that goods are new if they are deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, used, or secondhand. (e) Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade, or that goods are of a particular style or model, if they are of another. (f) Disparaging the goods, services, business, or reputation of another by false or misleading representation of fact. (g) Advertising or representing goods or services with intent not to dispose of those goods or services as advertised or represented. (h) Advertising goods or services with intent not to supply reasonably expectable public demand, unless the advertisement discloses a limitation of quantity in immediate conjunction with the advertised goods or services. (i) Making false or misleading statements of fact concerning the reasons for, existence of, or amounts of price reductions. (j) Representing that a part, replacement, or repair service is needed when it is not. (k) Representing to a party to whom goods
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts MCL 445.903(1)(t) regarding unfair trade practices. The original text ensures disputes are resolved according to Michigan law, while the changed text grants the Transporter sole discretion, potentially overriding legal protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'unfair, unconscionable, or deceptive methods, acts, or practices in the conduct of trade or commerce' as unlawful, and the modified text falls under this definition because it allows the Transporter to resolve disputes without regard to Michigan law, giving them an unfair advantage and potentially depriving the other party of their rights under the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall incorporate and in all respects be subject to the "General Terms and Conditions" and the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) set forth in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, as may be revised from time to time. Transporter may file and seek Commission approval under Section 4 of the Natural Gas Act (NGA) at any time and from time to time to change any rates, charges or provisions set forth in the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) and the "General Terms and Conditions" in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, and Transporter shall have the right to place such changes in effect in accordance with the NGA, and this Agreement shall be deemed to include such changes and any such changes Source: TC PIPELINES LP, 10-K, 2/26/2016 which become effective by operation of law and Commission Order, without prejudice to Shipper's right to protest the same.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall incorporate and in all respects be subject to the "General Terms and Conditions" and the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) set forth in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, as may be revised from time to time. Transporter may file and seek Commission approval under Section 4 of the Natural Gas Act (NGA) at any time and from time to time to change any rates, charges or provisions set forth in the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) and the "General Terms and Conditions" in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, and Transporter shall have the right to place such changes in effect in accordance with the NGA, and this Agreement shall be deemed to include such changes and any such changes Source: TC PIPELINES LP, 10-K, 2/26/2016 which become effective by operation of law and Commission Order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "without prejudice to Shipper's right to protest the same," the agreement now implies that the Shipper has no right to protest changes to the rates, charges, or provisions. This contradicts the Shipper's right to protest rate changes under the NGA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5(a) of the Natural Gas Act (15 U.S. Code § 717d(a)) grants any interested party, including shippers, the right to file a complaint with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) concerning rates or charges. Removing the language preserving the Shipper's right to protest effectively waives a legally protected right, contradicting this provision of the NGA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 717d - Fixing rates and charges; determination of cost of production or transportation | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 717d - Fixing rates and charges; determination of cost of production or transportation U.S. Code Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Decreases in rates Whenever the Commission , after a hearing had upon its own motion or upon complaint of any State, municipality , State commission , or gas distributing company, shall find that any rate, charge, or classification demanded, observed, charged, or collected by any natural-gas company in connection with any transportation or sale of natural gas, subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, or that any rule, regulation, practice, or contract affecting such rate, charge, or classification is unjust, unreasonable, unduly discriminatory, or preferential, the Commission shall determine the just and reasonable rate, charge, classification, rule, regulation, practice, or contract to be thereafter observed and in force, and shall fix the same by order: Provided, however, That the Commission shall have no power to order any increase in any rate contained in the currently effective schedule of such natural gas company on file with the Commission, unless such increase is in accordance with a new schedule filed by such natural gas company; but the Commission may order a decrease where existing rates are unjust, unduly discriminatory, preferential, otherwise unlawful, or are not the lowest reasonable rates. (b) Costs of production and transportation The Commission upon its own motion, or upon the request of any State commission , whenever it can do so without prejudice to the efficient and proper conduct of its affairs, may investigate and determine the cost of the production or transportation of natural gas by a natural-gas company in cases where the Commission has no autho
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the phrase "without prejudice to Shipper's right to protest the same." This creates a contradiction because Section 5(a) of the Natural Gas Act (NGA), as described in the Law Explanation and reflected in the scraped law snippets (15 USC 717d), grants any interested party, including shippers, the right to file a complaint with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) concerning rates or charges. Removing language that explicitly preserves the Shipper's right to protest implies a waiver of this right, which is directly contrary to the NGA's provision for complaint filings by any State, municipality, State commission, or gas distributing company as stated in snippet 2. The omission here, based on the perturbation explanation, creates a non-compliance with the NGA.

perturbation.original_text: 15. NOTICES AND COMMUNICATIONS: All notices and communications with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing by mail, e-mail, or fax, or other means as agreed to by the parties, and sent to the addresses stated below or to any other such address(es) as may be designated in writing by mail, e-mail, or fax, or other means similarly agreed to: ADMINISTRATIVE MATTERS Great Lakes Gas Transmission Limited Partnership Commercial Services 700 Louisiana St., Suite 700 Houston, TX 77002-2700 ANR PIPELINE COMPANY 700 Louisiana St., Suite 700 Houston, TX 77002-2700 Attn:
perturbation.changed_text: 15. NOTICES AND COMMUNICATIONS: All notices and communications with respect to this Agreement shall be at the discretion of Great Lakes Gas Transmission Limited Partnership.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the communication method to the discretion of one party, the agreement removes the assurance of written communication and agreed-upon addresses. This could potentially violate legal requirements for proper notice in contract disputes and regulatory matters.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-202 outlines requirements for providing notice, which often necessitates reasonable steps to inform the other party. Granting one party sole discretion over all notices and communications could potentially fall short of providing reasonable notice as defined under the UCC, creating a situation where legally required notifications are not properly delivered, potentially violating the agreement's intended purpose and the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has " notice " of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) " Knowledge " means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) " Discover ", " learn ", or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person " notifies " or " gives " a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person " receives " a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to k
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the assurance of written communication and agreed-upon addresses for notices, granting one party sole discretion. This contradicts the UCC § 1-202's requirement for 'taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course'. The original text ensures a defined method for communication, while the altered text allows a method that may not reasonably inform the other party, thus creating a contradiction.
